chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: CSCE 689
Course Title: Advanced Topics in Symbolic Artificial Intelligence
Section: 602
Time: MW 4:10-5:25
Location: 126 HRBB
Credit Hours: 3

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: Advanced topics in symbolic Artificial Intelligence, including algorithms for Inference, Planning, and Uncertainty Reasoning. This course will explore symbolic methods for knowledge representation, inference, and planning, to enable common-sense reasoning and high-level problem-solving in intelligent systems, for writing agent specifications, and for generating explanations for decisions ("explainable Al"). Topics include: use of Logic Programming for design of knowledge-based systems, non-monotonic logics (for reasoning with defaults/assumptions), temporal logics, and probabilistic inference (e.g. Bayesian Networks). The course spans a wide spectrum of concepts, from foundational knowledge (formal complexity and decidability theory), through algorithms with associated data-structures and heuristics, to practical Al tools (e.g., declarative programming languages). This course requires a knowledge of Al fundamentals, and builds on concepts that would be learned in a first-semester Al class.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
At the end of the course, students will be able to...
• Describe and implement various algorithms for logical inference, including unification, forward-chaining, backward-chaining, and resolution.
• Write programs in PROLOG to solve various types of problems, and understand how negation-as-failure and the cut operator work.
• Characterize the computational complexity of logical inference, and describe algorithmic heuristics for improving the efficiency of inference.
• Explain different methods for Default and Non-monotonic Reasoning, and its utility in building real-world knowledge-based systems.
• Describe and implement various algorithms for symbolic planning, including forward state-space search, goal regression, and partial order planning.
• Characterize the computational complexity of symbolic planning, and describe algorithmic heuristics for improving the efficiency of generating plans.
• Describe and implement methods for making probabilistic inferences with Bayesian Networks, and for solving Markov Decision Problems.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig, Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach (AIMA, hereafter), 4th Edition, Prentice Hall, New Jersey, 2020.
References to relevant research papers will be provided as additional reading material.

chunks.title: Description of Graded Work
chunks.content:
The coursework will consist of several programming assignments throughout the semester. Students will commit their code in a private repository on the student's Github account, with the instructor added as collaborator. Students may use any programming language they want, but they must be proficient at writing and debugging their own code. Programs will be evaluated based on ability to solve test cases and required capabilities as described in handouts. Code reviews and demonstration/evaluation will be done in 1-on-1 meetings with the instructor to be held in the middle of the semester and at the end, resulting in a written assessment and grade. Students will also be expected to keep up with reading assignments and participate in classroom discussions.
Final projects will be done individually on a topic of interest to the student that is relevant to the course, and must be pre-approved by the instructor. The final project report will be written-up in the format of research paper (with background, motivation, related work, description of methods/implementation, and results or demonstrations), to be submitted during finals week.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Programming assignments: 80%.
• Final project: 20%
• The cutoff for an 'A' will be >= 90% of total score, >= 80% for a 'B', >= 70% for a `C', >= 60% for a 'D', and below 60% for an 'F'.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late penalty: -10% per day late (prorated over 24 hours).
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Table 1: weekly schedule
| Week | Topic | Programming assignment |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inference in First-Order Logic: algorithms for unification, forward-chaining (Rete), and backward chaining | implement unification |
| 2 | Computational complexity of inference (Cook's Theorem, QBFs, Semi-decidability of FOL) | implement backward-chaining |
| 3 | Resolution Refutation; heuristics | implement resolution |
| 4 | PROLOG; negation-as-failure; cut operator; different types of applications/programming paradigms | solve practice problems with PROLOG |
| 5 | Default Reasoning and Non-monotonic Logic (circumscription, fixed-point semantics/stable models; Answer-Set Programming; Truth Maintenance Systems; abductive reasoning) | write a program in CLIPS |
| 6 | Description Logics, Ontologies, Semantic Web; OWL/RDF | write a knowledge base in Protege |
| 7 | Modal logics; possible-worlds semantics; temporal logics, model-checking; Interval Logic | (midterm code reviews and evaluation) |
| 8 | Planning: Representation of Actions (PDDL and Situation Calculus); SatPlan | implement SatPlan using a Boolean Sat solver |
| 9 | Forward State-Space Search; FF; heuristics | generate plans with PDDL via BFS and A* |
| 10 | Goal Regression | implement Goal Regression |
| 11 | Complexity of Planning; Abstraction Planning | use GOLOG to infer plans from Sit Calc axioms |
| 12 | POP, GraphPlan | implement POP |
| 13 | Algorithms for probabilistic inferences with Bayesian Networks (Variable Elimination; Belief Propagation) | (final code reviews and evaluation) |
| 14 | Algorithms for solving Markov Decision Problems (Policy and Value Iteration) | |
| 15 | (finals week) | (final projects due) |

chunks.title: Use of AI Technologies like ChatGPT
chunks.content: This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students themselves. Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any substantive portion of an assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write assignments and using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT or GitHub Copilot.

boilerplate_policies: Civil Rights, Free Speech, and Title IX Policies
Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)

warnings: Schedule has no specific dates, only weeks.
